children.text: While living here at the homeless shelter, I’ve been taking essentially free courses through

and I’ve flown through them happily, securing a 4.0 GPA and 24 college credits. However, as I am continuing to pursue reunification with my family, I am remaining sensitive to the dynamics of the situation and what may or may not need to change. I love college and I don’t want to drop out completely. Thus, I am considering transferring from Maestro to WGU because WGU is regionally accredited, obtaining a degree should be a speedier process, and the projected financials are also in favor of WGU.

My research has indicated that regional accreditation is generally considered stronger than national accreditation, and it was only recently that I realized Maestro is nationally accredited rather than regionally accredited. The difference between the two systems is nuanced, but it matters for how degrees are viewed. If I ever want to be a respected member of the academic community, I would prefer to hold a degree from a regionally accredited institution. Yet, if this were the only pull toward WGU, I would probably stick with Maestro and instead argue that national accreditation should be more highly valued.

Additionally, I suspect that I will be able to become a degreed professional at a quicker pace at WGU. First there’s the matter of my previous college credits that Maestro was unable to transfer over, which will probably be counted by WGU; and second, I’ve been told that WGU lets you take an entrance exam per course and, if you do well enough at that, a final exam right afterwards. These two points weigh heavily on the scales, as it’s a pity to lose my old credits and I am a bit of an autodidact. Still, with just these factors, I’d still be staying put but for the final piece of the puzzle.

My wife is making by all accounts decent money as a special ed teacher, but I am always wishing I could be contributing financially to the family; however, my financial contribution is limited while I am studying at Maestro, whereas at WGU, I could be adding to the family coffers. If I do get back to work and living with my wife and children, our income would very likely exceed the $100,000 per year household maximum for the Maestro scholarship; whereas at WGU, the costs are minimal even without a scholarship. You see, once I reach junior status, I become eligible for a plethora of remote internships that all pay from $25-$35 per hour, which would more than offset the transition from free to paid college. With this, I feel that I am very well primed for a move toward WGU.

I’d like to transfer from Maestro to WGU for three main reasons — accreditation, speed, and money. But it’s more than that. Transferring to WGU represents a shift in the overall scheme of life I’m living — it represents moving back in with my family and ensuring we have more than we need to get by. The transfer isn’t about petty competition between institutions, but about finding the right fit from myriad possible directions.
